1.o: We have David sinking under infirmities. He was chastised for his recent sins, and felt the effects of his former toils and hardships.
1.m: David was feeling sick because he had been punished for his recent sins, and he was also tired from the hard work and difficulties he faced before.

1.o: Indulgent parents are often chastised with disobedient children, who are anxious to possess their estates. No worldly wisdom, nor experience, nor sacredness of character, can insure the continuance in any former course of those who remain under the power of self-love. But we may well wonder by what arts Joab and Abiathar could be drawn aside.
1.m:
Parents who are too lenient often have disobedient children who want to inherit their property. 

No amount of worldly knowledge, experience, or good character can guarantee that someone will keep following the same path if they are controlled by selfish desires.

It’s surprising how Joab and Abiathar were led astray by certain methods.

1.o: Observe Nathan's address to Bathsheba. Let me give thee counsel how to save thy own life, and the life of thy son. Such as this is the counsel Christ's ministers give us in his name, to give all diligence, not only that no man take our crown, Re 3:11, but that we save our lives, even the lives of our souls. David made a solemn declaration of his firm cleaving to his former resolution, that Solomon should be his successor. Even the recollection of the distresses from which the Lord redeemed him, increased his comfort, inspired his hopes, and animated him to his duty, under the decays of nature and the approach of death.
1.m:
Notice how Nathan spoke to Bathsheba. He told her how to protect herself and her son. This is what Christ’s followers tell us—to work hard so no one takes away our reward and to save ourselves, even our very souls. 

David promised again that he would keep his earlier decision for Solomon to be his heir. Remembering how God helped him out of trouble gave David joy, hope, and strength to do his duties as he grew old and was close to dying.

1.o: The people expressed great joy and satisfaction in the elevation of Solomon. Every true Israelite rejoices in the exaltation of the Son of David. Combinations formed upon evil principles will soon be dissolved, when self-interest calls another way. How can those who do evil deeds expect to have good tidings? Adonijah had despised Solomon, but soon dreaded him. We see here, as in a glass, Jesus, the Son of David and the Son of God, exalted to the throne of glory, notwithstanding all his enemies. His kingdom is far greater than that of his father David, and therein all the true people of God cordially rejoice. The prosperity of his cause is vexation and terror to his enemies. No horns of the altar, nor forms of godliness, nor pretences to religion, can profit those who will not submit to His authority, and accept of his salvation; and if their submission be hypocritical, they shall perish without remedy.
1.m:
The people were very happy about Solomon becoming king. True followers of Israel are glad when the Son of David rises to power. Evil plans don’t last long when selfish motives lead people elsewhere. Can those who do wrong expect good news?

Adonijah once looked down on Solomon but now fears him. This shows us Jesus, the Son of David and Son of God, being raised up despite all his enemies. His kingdom is much bigger than his father David’s, and all true believers cheer for it. 

His success troubles and scares his opponents. Pretending to follow God won't help those who refuse to obey Him. Even fake obedience won’t save them from punishment.

2.o: David's charge to Solomon is, to keep the charge of the Lord. The authority of a dying father is much, but nothing to that of a living God. God promised David that the Messiah should come from his descendants, and that promise was absolute; but the promise, that there should not fail of them a man on the throne of Israel, was conditional; if he walks before God in sincerity, with zeal and resolution: in order hereunto, he must take heed to his way. (1Ki 2:5-11)
2.m: David told Solomon to follow God’s commands. A father’s authority is important, but God’s rules are even more powerful. God promised David that one of his family would be the Messiah. This promise was sure. But God also said that someone from David’s family would always rule Israel, as long as they were faithful and sincere, walking closely with God. To do this, Solomon needed to watch his actions carefully. (1Ki 2:5-11)

2.o: These dying counsels concerning Joab and Shimei, did not come from personal anger, but for the security of Solomon's throne, which was the murders he had committed, but would readily repeat them to carry any purpose; though long reprieved, he shall be reckoned with at last. Time does not wear out the guilt of any sin, particularly of murder. Concerning Shimei, Hold him not guiltless; do not think him any true friend to thee, or thy government, or fit to be trusted; he has no less malice now than he had then. David's dying sentiments are recorded, as delivered under the influence of the Holy Ghost, 2Sa 23:1–7. The Lord discovered to him the offices and the salvation of that glorious personage, the Messiah, whose coming he then foretold, and from whom he derived all his comforts and expectations. That passage gives a decided proof that David died under the influence of the Holy Ghost, in the exercise of faith and hope.
2.m:
These final instructions about Joab and Shimei were not because of personal anger, but to protect Solomon’s rule. Joab had killed people before and might do it again if given the chance. Even after waiting a long time, he will still face punishment eventually. Time doesn’t erase the guilt of sins, especially murder.

About Shimei, don't consider him innocent or trustworthy. He still has the same hatred now as before. 

David’s thoughts as he died are written down because they came from the Holy Spirit. In 2 Samuel 23:1–7, God showed David the role and future of the Messiah. This helped David see where his comfort and hopes lay. This shows that David spoke these words while filled with the Holy Spirit, full of faith and hope.

2.o: Solomon received Bathsheba with all the respect that was owing to a mother; but let none be asked for that which they ought not to grant. It ill becomes a good man to prefer a bad request, or to appear in a bad cause. According to eastern customs it was plain that Adonijah sought to be king, by his asking for Abishag as his wife, and Solomon could not be safe while he lived. Ambitious, turbulent spirits commonly prepare death for themselves. Many a head has been lost by catching at a crown.
2.m:
Solomon treated Bathsheba with all the respect she deserved as a mother. But no one should ask for things they shouldn’t get. 

It’s wrong for a good person to make an unfair request or support a bad cause. In those days, asking for Abishag to be his wife clearly showed that Adonijah wanted to become king. 

Solomon couldn’t feel safe if Adonijah was alive. People who want power often end up causing their own deaths. Many have lost their lives trying to grab a crown.

2.o: Solomon's words to Abiathar, and his silence, imply that some recent conspiracies had been entered into. Those that show kindness to God's people shall have it remembered to their advantage. For this reason Solomon spares Abiathar's life, but dismisses him from his offices. In case of such sins as the blood of beasts would atone for, the altar was a refuge, but not in Joab's case. Solomon looks upward to God as the Author of peace, and forward to eternity as the perfection of it. The Lord of peace himself gives us that peace which is everlasting.
2.m:
Solomon's words to Abiathar and his silence suggest there were some recent secret plans against him. People who help God’s followers will be rewarded. Because of this, Solomon lets Abiathar live but removes him from his jobs.

For smaller sins, sacrifices on the altar could forgive someone. But Joab’s sin was different. 

Solomon looked up to God as the source of all peace. He also thought about eternal peace in heaven. The Lord of peace gives us true, lasting peace.

2.o: The old malignity remains in the unconverted heart, and a watchful eye should be kept on those who, like Shimei, have manifested their enmity, but have given no evidence of repentance. No engagements or dangers will restrain worldly men; they go on, though they forfeit their lives and souls. Let us remember, God will not accommodate his judgment to us. His eye is over us; and let us strive to walk as in his presence. Let our every act, word, and thought, be governed by this great truth, that the hour is quickly coming when the smallest circumstances of our lives shall be brought to light, and our eternal state be fixed by a righteous and unerring God. Thus Solomon's throne was established in peace, as the type of the Redeemer's kingdom of peace and righteousness. And it is a comfort, in reference to the enmity of the church's enemies, that, how much soever they rage, it is a vain thing they imagine. Christ's throne is established, and they cannot shake it.
2.m:
The evil nature stays in people who haven't changed their hearts. We need to keep an eye on those who show hate, like Shimei did, without showing any regret. Worldly people won’t stop even if they risk losing their lives or souls. 

Remember, God doesn’t adjust his judgments for us. He watches us all the time, so we should try to live as if he’s always there. Our actions, words, and thoughts should follow this rule because soon everything about our lives will be revealed, and God will decide our eternal fate fairly and accurately.

Solomon’s peaceful reign showed what the Messiah’s kingdom would be like—full of peace and justice. It’s comforting to know that even when the church’s enemies cause trouble, it’s useless because Jesus’ throne is secure and nothing can shake it.

3.o: He that loved the Lord, should, for his sake, have fixed his love upon one of the Lord's people. Solomon was a wise man, a rich man, a great man; yet the brightest praise of him, is that which is the character of all the saints, even the poorest, “He loved the Lord.” Where God sows plentifully, he expects to reap accordingly; and those that truly love God and his worship, will not grudge the expenses of their religion. We must never think that wasted which is laid out in the service of God.
3.m:
Anyone who loves the Lord should also love one of the Lord’s people because of Him. 

Solomon was very smart, wealthy, and important. But his greatest honor was loving the Lord, just like all believers do, whether they are poor or not.

When God gives us much, He expects us to give back generously. People who really love God and His worship won’t mind spending money on religious needs.

We shouldn’t see anything spent on serving God as wasted.

3.o: Solomon's dream was not a common one. While his bodily powers were locked up in sleep, the powers of his soul were strengthened; he was enabled to receive the Divine vision, and to make a suitable choice. God, in like manner, puts us in the ready way to be happy, by assuring us we shall have what we need, and pray for. Solomon's making such a choice when asleep, and the powers of reason least active, showed it came from the grace of God. Having a humble sense of his own wants and weakness, he pleads, Lord, I am but a little child. The more wise and considerate men are, the better acquainted they are with their own weakness, and the more jealous of themselves. Solomon begs of God to give him wisdom. We must pray for it, Jas 1:5, that it may help us in our particular calling, and the various occasions we have. Those are accepted of God, who prefer spiritual blessings to earthly good. It was a prevailing prayer, and prevailed for more than he asked. God gave him wisdom, such as no other prince was ever blessed with; and also gave him riches and honour. If we make sure of wisdom and grace, these will bring outward prosperity with them, or sweeten the want of it. The way to get spiritual blessings, is to wrestle with God in prayer for them. The way to get earthly blessings, is to refer ourselves to God concerning them. Solomon has wisdom given him, because he did ask it, and wealth, because he did not.
3.m:
Solomon had an unusual dream while sleeping. His mind was awake even though his body was resting. He received a special message from God and made a good choice. 

God helps us find happiness by promising us what we need if we ask. Solomon’s request during his sleep shows that it came from God’s kindness. Knowing his own weaknesses, Solomon said, “Lord, I’m just a young man.” Wise people know they aren’t perfect and trust God more.

He asked God for wisdom. Just like James 1:5 tells us, we should pray for wisdom to guide us in our work and daily life. People who value spiritual gifts over material things are favored by God.

His prayer worked well. Not only did God grant him wisdom, but also more than he requested. Solomon became wiser than any other king. God also gave him great wealth and honor. When we focus on getting wisdom and grace, we often gain worldly success too, or at least enjoy what we do have.

To get spiritual blessings, we must pray earnestly. For earthly blessings, we should leave it to God. Solomon got wisdom because he asked for it, and he got wealth because he didn't ask for it directly.

3.o: An instance of Solomon's wisdom is given. Notice the difficulty of the case. To find out the true mother, he could not try which the child loved best, and therefore tried which loved the child best: the mother's sincerity will be tried, when the child is in danger. Let parents show their love to their children, especially by taking care of their souls, and snatching them as brands out of the burning. By this and other instances of the wisdom with which God endued him, Solomon had great reputation among his people. This was better to him than weapons of war; for this he was both feared and loved.
3.m:
An example of Solomon's wisdom is shown here. Notice how hard the situation was. To figure out who the real mother was, he couldn't see which woman liked the baby more. Instead, he saw which one cared most about the baby: the real mom would show her true feelings when the baby was in danger. Parents should show their love for their kids by protecting them, especially from harm. 

Solomon gained a lot of respect because of things like this. His wisdom made him famous among his people. It helped him more than having weapons. People both feared and loved him because of his wise actions.

4.o: In the choice of the great officers of Solomon's court, no doubt, his wisdom appeared. Several are the same that were in his father's time. A plan was settled by which no part of the country was exhausted to supply his court, though each sent its portion.
4.m: Solomon showed his wisdom in choosing the important people for his court. Many of these officials were the same ones who worked during his father’s time. They had a system where different parts of the country provided some help, so no area felt overburdened.

4.o: Never did the crown of Israel shine so bright, as when Solomon wore it. He had peace on all sides. Herein, his kingdom was a type of the Messiah's; for to Him it is promised that he shall have the heathen for his inheritance, and that princes shall worship him. The spiritual peace, and joy, and holy security, of all the faithful subjects of the Lord Jesus, were typified by that of Israel. The kingdom of God is not, as Solomon's was, meat and drink, but, what is infinitely better, righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost. The vast number of his attendants, and the great resort to him, are shown by the provision daily made. Herein Christ far outdoes Solomon, that he feeds all his subjects, not with the bread that perishes, but with that which endures to eternal life.
4.m:
Solomon ruled Israel during its brightest time. His kingdom enjoyed peace from all sides. This was like a picture of the coming kingdom of the Messiah. It was promised that the Messiah would rule over all nations and kings would bow down to him. 

The peace, joy, and safety of those who follow Jesus today remind us of the peace Solomon brought to Israel. Unlike Solomon’s kingdom, which offered food and drink, Jesus’ kingdom gives people something much better—righteousness, peace, and joy from the Holy Spirit.

Many people came to see Solomon each day because of the large amount of food prepared. But Jesus goes beyond even that. He provides for all his followers with food that lasts forever, not just for a short time.

4.o: Solomon's wisdom was more his glory than his wealth. He had what is here called largeness of heart, for the heart is often put for the powers of the mind. He had the gift of utterance, as well as wisdom. It is very desirable, that those who have large gifts of any kind, should have large hearts to use them for the good of others. What treasures of wisdom and knowledge are lost! But every sort of knowledge that is needful for salvation is to be found in the holy Scriptures. There came persons from all parts, who were more eager after knowledge than their neighbours, to hear the wisdom of Solomon. Solomon was herein a type of Christ, in whom are hid all treasures of wisdom and knowledge; and hid for us, for he is made of God to us, wisdom. Christ's fame shall spread through all the earth, and men of all nations shall come to him, learn of him, and take upon them his easy yoke, and find rest for their souls.
4.m:
Solomon's wisdom was more impressive than his wealth. He had what people call a big heart, which means he used his mind well. He could speak clearly too. It’s great when smart people share their skills to help others. Many valuable ideas get lost, but everything needed for salvation is in the Holy Scriptures. People traveled far to hear Solomon because they wanted to learn. 

Solomon was like Jesus, who has all the world’s wisdom and knowledge hidden within him. This wisdom is meant for everyone. Jesus’ teachings will reach everywhere, and people from different countries will come to him. They’ll learn from him, accept his guidance, and find peace for their souls.

5.o: Here is Solomon's design to build a temple. There is no adversary, no Satan, so the word is; no instrument of Satan to oppose it, or to divert from it. Satan does all he can, to hinder temple work. When there is no evil abroad, then let us be ready and active in that which is good, and get forward. Let God's promises quicken our endeavours. And all outward skill and advantages should be made serviceable to the interests of Christ's kingdom.—If Tyre supplies Israel with craftsmen, Israel will supply Tyre with corn, Eze 27:17. Thus, by the wise disposal of Providence, one country has need of another, and is benefitted by another, that there may be dependence on one another, to the glory of God.
5.m:
Solomon wanted to build a temple. The word says there was no enemy or devil trying to stop him. Satan tries his best to prevent such holy work. When nothing bad happens, we should do more good things and move ahead. God’s promises should encourage us to try harder. We should use all our skills and advantages to help spread Christ’s message.

Tyre sent skilled workers to Israel, and in return, Israel gave Tyre food, as mentioned in Ezekiel 27:17. This shows how different places depend on each other through God’s plan, bringing honor to Him.

5.o: The temple was chiefly built by the riches and labour of Gentiles, which typified their being called into the church. Solomon commanded, and they brought costly stones for the foundation. Christ, who is laid for a Foundation, is a chosen and precious Stone. We should lay our foundation firm, and bestow most pains on that part of our religion which lies out of the sight of men. And happy those who, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, for a habitation of God through the Spirit. Who among us will build in the house of the Lord?
5.m: The temple was mainly built using money and work from non-Jewish people, which symbolizes them joining the church. Solomon told them to bring expensive stones for the foundation. Jesus, who is the foundation, is a chosen and valuable stone. We should make sure our foundations are strong and put extra effort into parts of our faith that others don’t see. Those who become living stones and help build a spiritual home for God are very fortunate. Who among us will contribute to building God’s house?

6.o: The temple is called the house of the Lord, because it was directed and modelled by him, and was to be employed in his service. This gave it the beauty of holiness, that it was the house of the Lord, which was far beyond all other beauties. It was to be the temple of the God of peace, therefore no iron tool must be heard; quietness and silence suit and help religious exercises. God's work should be done with much care and little noise. Clamour and violence often hinder, but never further the work of God. Thus the kingdom of God in the heart of man grows up in silence, Mr 5:27.
6.m:
The temple is called the house of the Lord because he designed and guided its construction, and it was meant for his service. Its true beauty came from being the house of the Lord, which was more important than any physical beauty. 

Since it was to be a place for peaceful worship, no noisy tools were allowed. Quietness fits well with religious activities. God’s work should be done carefully and quietly. Loud noises and arguments can stop God’s work. They never help it along.

Just as the kingdom of God within people grows silently, so did this temple. For example, see Mark 5:27.

6.o: None employ themselves for God, without having his eye upon them. But God plainly let Solomon know that all the charge for building this temple, would neither excuse from obedience to the law of God, nor shelter from his judgments, in case of disobedience.
6.m: No one works for God without him watching them. But God made it clear to Solomon that paying for the temple’s construction wouldn’t excuse him from following God’s laws. It also wouldn’t protect him from God’s punishments if he disobeyed.

6.o: See what was typified by this temple. 1. Christ is the true Temple. In him dwells all the fulness of the Godhead; in him meet all God's spiritual Israel; through him we have access with confidence to God. 2. Every believer is a living temple, in whom the Spirit of God dwells, 1Co 3:16. This living temple is built upon Christ as its Foundation, and will be perfect in due time. 3. The gospel church is the mystical temple. It grows to a holy temple in the Lord, enriched and beautified with the gifts and graces of the Spirit. This temple is built firm, upon a Rock. 4. Heaven is the everlasting temple. There the church will be fixed. All that shall be stones in that building, must, in the present state of preparation, be fitted and made ready for it. Let sinners come to Jesus as the living Foundation, that they may be built on him, a part of this spiritual house, consecrated in body and soul to the glory of God.
6.m:
Here’s the passage rewritten in simpler, clearer language:

---

Think about what the temple represents. 

1. Christ is the real temple. He has all of God within Him. People who follow God find their home in Christ. Through Christ, we can approach God confidently.

2. Each person who believes in God is like a living temple where the Holy Spirit lives. These believers build their faith on Christ, who is the foundation. Over time, these people become complete.

3. The Christian church acts like a spiritual temple. It grows stronger and more beautiful because of the gifts and abilities given by the Holy Spirit. This temple stands solidly on a rock.

4. Heaven is the eternal temple. That’s where the church will finally settle. To be part of this heavenly building, people need to get ready now. Sinners should turn to Jesus, who is the strong base. By doing so, they can join His spiritual family and dedicate themselves fully to honor God.

---

7.o: All Solomon's buildings, though beautiful, were intended for use. Solomon began with the temple; he built for God first, and then his other buildings. The surest foundations of lasting prosperity are laid in early piety. He was thirteen years building his house, yet he built the temple in little more than seven years; not that he was more exact, but less eager in building his own house, than in building God's. We ought to prefer God's honour before our own ease and satisfaction.
7.m:
All of Solomon's buildings, even though they were very nice, had practical purposes. Solomon started by building the temple. He made a place for God first, and then he worked on his other buildings. 

The best way to have long-lasting success is to start with being religious from an early age. It took Solomon thirteen years to build his own house, but he finished the temple in just over seven years. This wasn't because he was more careful, but because he cared less about building his own home compared to building God’s house.

We should put God's honor above our own comfort and happiness.

7.o: The two brazen pillars in the porch of the temple, some think, were to teach those that came to worship, to depend upon God only, for strength and establishment in all their religious exercises. “Jachin,” God will fix this roving mind. It is good that the heart be established with grace. “Boaz,” In him is our strength, who works in us both to will and to do. Spiritual strength and stability are found at the door of God's temple, where we must wait for the gifts of grace, in use of the means of grace. Spiritual priests and spiritual sacrifices must be washed in the laver of Christ's blood, and of regeneration. We must wash often, for we daily contract pollution. There are full means provided for our cleansing; so that if we have our lot for ever among the unclean it will be our own fault. Let us bless God for the fountain opened by the sacrifice of Christ for sin and for uncleanness.
7.m:
Two bronze pillars stood in front of the temple. Some people think these taught worshippers to rely on God alone for strength and support during their prayers.

“Jachin” means God will steady our wandering thoughts. It’s important to set our hearts firmly with God’s help.

“Boaz” reminds us that our strength comes from Him, who helps us want to do what is right and actually do it.

True spiritual power and stability come from God’s house. We need to seek His grace through prayer and other ways He provides.

Spiritual leaders and offerings must be cleansed in the basin filled with Jesus’ blood and the gift of new life.

We should keep washing ourselves because we get dirty each day. Plenty of ways exist to clean us, so if we stay impure, it’s our own choice.

Thank God for the way Jesus’ sacrifice opens up cleansing for sins and dirtiness.

7.o: Christ is now the Temple and the Builder; the Altar and the Sacrifice; the Light of our souls, and the Bread of life; able to supply all the wants of all that have applied or shall apply to him. Outward images cannot represent, words cannot express, the heart cannot conceive, his preciousness or his love. Let us come to him, and wash away our sins in his blood; let us seek for the purifying grace of his Spirit; let us maintain communion with the Father through his intercession, and yield up ourselves and all we have to his service. Being strengthened by him, we shall be accepted, useful, and happy.
7.m:
Christ is now the temple and the builder; he is the altar and the sacrifice; he is the light of our souls and the bread of life. He can meet all the needs of those who turn to him. Pictures cannot show how wonderful he is, words cannot describe his beauty, and hearts cannot fully grasp his love. 

Let’s go to him to cleanse our sins with his blood. We should ask for the cleansing power of his Spirit. We should stay connected to the Father through Christ’s prayers. And we should give ourselves and everything we have to serve him.

By his strength, we will be accepted, helpful, and happy.

8.o: The bringing in the ark, is the end which must crown the work: this was done with great solemnity. The ark was fixed in the place appointed for its rest in the inner part of the house, whence they expected God to speak to them, even in the most holy place. The staves of the ark were drawn out, so as to direct the high priest to the mercy-seat over the ark, when he went in, once a year, to sprinkle the blood there; so that they continued of use, though there was no longer occasion to carry it by them. The glory of God appearing in a cloud may signify, 1. The darkness of that dispensation, in comparison with the light of the gospel, by which, with open face, we behold, as in a glass, the glory of the Lord. 2. The darkness of our present state, in comparison with the sight of God, which will be the happiness of heaven, where the Divine glory is unveiled.
8.m:
Bringing in the ark was the final goal of their work. They did this with great ceremony. 

They placed the ark in the spot chosen for it inside the house. This was where they thought God would speak to them, from the most holy room.

The poles used to carry the ark were left extended. This helped the high priest find the mercy seat above the ark each year when he sprinkled blood there. Even though they didn't need to move the ark anymore, the poles still served a purpose.

When God’s glory appeared in a cloud, it could mean two things:

First, that time was darker compared to the bright light of the gospel, through which we now see God clearly, like looking at ourselves in a mirror.

Second, that our current situation is dark compared to seeing God directly, which will be the joy of heaven, where God’s glory shines without hiding.

8.o: Solomon encouraged the priests, who were much astonished at the dark cloud. The dark dispensations of Providence should quicken us in fleeing for refuge to the hope of the gospel. Nothing can more reconcile us to them, than to consider what God has said, and to compare his word and works together. Whatever good we do, we must look on it as the performance of God's promise to us, not of our promises to him.
8.m: Solomon encouraged the priests, who were very surprised by the dark cloud. Dark times from God should remind us to turn to the hope given in the gospel. Thinking about what God has promised helps us accept those hard times better. We should see how his words match what he does. When we do good things, we need to think of them as fulfilling God’s promises to us, not just our promises to him.

8.o: In this excellent prayer, Solomon does as we should do in every prayer; he gives glory to God. Fresh experiences of the truth of God's promises call for larger praises. He sues for grace and favour from God. The experiences we have of God's performing his promises, should encourage us to depend upon them, and to plead them with him; and those who expect further mercies, must be thankful for former mercies. God's promises must be the guide of our desires, and the ground of our hopes and expectations in prayer. The sacrifices, the incense, and the whole service of the temple, were all typical of the Redeemer's offices, oblation, and intercession. The temple, therefore, was continually to be remembered. Under one word, “forgive,” Solomon expressed all that he could ask in behalf of his people. For, as all misery springs from sin, forgiveness of sin prepares the way for the removal of every evil, and the receiving of every good. Without it, no deliverance can prove a blessing. In addition to the teaching of the word of God, Solomon entreated the Lord himself to teach the people to profit by all, even by their chastisements. They shall know every man the plague of his own heart, what it is that pains him; and shall spread their hands in prayer toward this house; whether the trouble be of body or mind, they shall represent it before God. Inward burdens seem especially meant. Sin is the plague of our own hearts; our in-dwelling corruptions are our spiritual diseases: every true Israelite endeavours to know these, that he may mortify them, and watch against the risings of them. These drive him to his knees; lamenting these, he spreads forth his hands in prayer. After many particulars, Solomon concludes with the general request, that God would hearken to his praying people. No place, now, under the gospel, can add to the prayers made in or towards it. The substance is Christ; whatever we ask in his name, it shall be given us. In this manner the Israel of God is established and sanctified, the backslider is recovered and healed. In this manner the stranger is brought nigh, the mourner is comforted, the name of God is glorified. Sin is the cause of all our troubles; repentance and forgiveness lead to all human happiness.
8.m:
In this great prayer, Solomon shows us how to pray. He gives praise to God. When we see God keep his promises, we should give more thanks. We should trust in God’s promises and use them to ask for help. 

The work at the temple, including the sacrifices and the burning of incense, pointed to Jesus. So the temple always reminded people about Jesus. 

Solomon asks God to forgive sins because without forgiveness, nothing else helps. Forgiveness clears the way for other blessings. 

He asks God to teach people to learn from both good times and hard times. People need to know their own faults so they can pray about them. Sins are inside us, causing problems. True believers try to stop these sins and guard against them. This makes them pray more.

After listing many requests, Solomon ends by asking God to listen to his people. Today, Christians pray in Jesus’ name, and God hears them. Through Jesus, God strengthens and purifies his people, brings back straying ones, and comforts those who mourn. Sin causes all our problems, but if we turn away from sin and get forgiven, we find real joy.

8.o: Never was a congregation dismissed with what was more likely to affect them, and to abide with them. What Solomon asks for in this prayer, is still granted in the intercession of Christ, of which his supplication was a type. We shall receive grace sufficient, suitable, and seasonable, in every time of need. No human heart is of itself willing to obey the gospel call to repentance, faith, and newness of life, walking in all the commandments of the Lord, yet Solomon exhorts the people to be perfect. This is the scriptural method, it is our duty to obey the command of the law and the call of the gospel, seeing we have broken the law. When our hearts are inclined thereto, feeling our sinfulness and weakness, we pray for Divine assistance; thus are we made able to serve God through Jesus Christ.
8.m:
Never were people sent away with words more likely to stay with them. 

What Solomon asked for in this prayer is still given today through Christ’s prayers. He helps us get just enough grace at the right times.

No one wants to follow God’s commands on their own. But Solomon tells the people to try anyway. It’s our job to listen to both God’s laws and His message of forgiveness. Since we’ve disobeyed God, we should do what He says.

When we feel weak and sinful, we ask God for help. Then He makes us strong enough to serve Him through Jesus.

8.o: Solomon offered a great sacrifice. He kept the feast of tabernacles, as it seems, after the feast of dedication. Thus should we go home, rejoicing, from holy ordinances, thankful for God's Goodness
8.m: Solomon made a big sacrifice. It looks like he celebrated the Feast of Tabernacles after the Feast of Dedication. We should return home happily after attending religious ceremonies, grateful for God’s goodness.

9.o: God warned Solomon, now he had newly built and dedicated the temple, that he and his people might not be high-minded, but fear. After all the services we can perform, we stand upon the same terms with the Lord as before. Nothing can purchase for us liberty to sin, nor would the true believer desire such a licence. He would rather be chastened of the Lord, than be allowed to go on with ease and prosperity in sin.
9.m: God told Solomon that after building and dedicating the temple, he should not become proud but stay humble and afraid of God. Even after doing good things, we are still on the same footing with God as before. Nothing we do can give us permission to sin. A true believer wouldn’t want that kind of freedom anyway. They would prefer to face God’s discipline rather than continue sinning without consequences.

9.o: Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities. Hiram did not like them. If Solomon would gratify him, let it be in his own element, by becoming his partner in trade, as he did. See how the providence of God suits this earth to the various tempers of men, and the dispositions of men to the earth, and all for the good of mankind in general.
9.m:
Solomon gave Hiram twenty cities. But Hiram didn’t like them. 

If Solomon wanted to please him, he should do it through trade instead. This way, Hiram could work alongside Solomon in business, just as he had before.

Notice how God’s plans fit people’s different personalities and preferences. It shows how everything works together for everyone’s benefit.

9.o: Here is a further account of Solomon's greatness. He began at the right end, for he built God's house first, and finished that before he began his own; then God blessed him, and he prospered in all his other buildings. Let piety begin, and profit follow; leave pleasure to the last. Whatever pains we take for the glory of God, and to profit others, we are likely to have the advantage. Canaan, the holy land, the glory of all lands, had no gold in it; which shows that the best produce is that which is for the present support of life, our own and others; such things did Canaan produce. Solomon got much by his merchandise, and yet has directed us to a better trade, within reach of the poorest. Wisdom is better than the merchandise of silver, and the gain thereof than fine gold, Pr 3:14.
9.m:
Here is more about Solomon’s greatness. He started by building God’s temple first, finishing that before he built his own palace. Then God blessed him, and he succeeded in all his other projects too. 

Let faith come first, followed by success. Whatever effort you put into honoring God and helping others will pay off. The land of Canaan, known as the holy land and the most beautiful place on earth, didn’t have any gold. This shows that the best resources are those that help sustain life, both yours and others’. 

Solomon made money from trading, but he taught us a better way to earn what really matters. Wisdom is worth more than silver, and its benefits are greater than gold, as Proverbs 3:14 tells us.

10.o: The queen of Sheba came to Solomon to hear his wisdom, thereby to improve her own. Our Saviour mentions her inquiries after God, by Solomon, as showing the stupidity of those who inquire not after God, by our Lord Jesus Christ. By waiting and prayer, by diligently searching the Scriptures, by consulting wise and experienced Christians, and by practising what we have learned, we shall be delivered from difficulties. Solomon's wisdom made more impression upon the queen of Sheba than all his prosperity and grandeur. There is a spiritual excellence in heavenly things, and in consistent Christians, to which no reports can do justice. Here the truth exceeded; and all who, through grace, are brought to commune with God, will say the one half was not told them of the pleasures and the advantages of wisdom's ways. Glorified saints, much more, will say of heaven, that the thousandth part was not told them, 1Co 2:9. She pronounced them happy that constantly attended Solomon. With much more reason may we say of Christ's servants, Blessed are they that dwell in his house; they will be still praising him. She made a noble present to Solomon. What we present to Christ, he needs not, but will have us do so to express our gratitude. The believer who has been with Jesus, will return to his station, discharge his duties with readiness, and from better motives; looking forward to the day when, being absent from the body, he shall be present with the Lord.
10.m:
The queen of Sheba visited Solomon to learn about his wisdom. Our Savior talked about her seeking knowledge from Solomon, pointing out how foolish people are for not asking Jesus about God. We can overcome problems by praying, studying the Bible, talking to wise believers, and putting what we learn into practice.

Solomon’s wisdom impressed the queen more than his wealth and power. Heavenly things and faithful Christians are even more amazing than anyone could describe. People who talk to God often say that words cannot fully explain the joy and benefits of following wisdom. Even more so, those in heaven might say that nothing can truly describe its wonders, as mentioned in 1 Corinthians 2:9.

She said that those who spent time with Solomon were blessed. Just as she praised those around Solomon, we should praise those who serve Christ. They live happily in His presence. 

The queen gave Solomon valuable gifts. While Jesus doesn’t need anything, giving Him thanks shows our appreciation. Someone who spends time with Jesus will eagerly do their duties and look forward to joining Him someday.

10.o: Solomon increased his wealth. Silver was nothing accounted of. Such is the nature of worldly wealth, plenty of it makes it the less valuable; much more should the enjoyment of spiritual riches lessen our esteem of all earthly possessions. If gold in abundance makes silver to be despised, shall not wisdom, and grace, and the foretastes of heaven, which are far better than gold, make gold to be lightly esteemed? See in Solomon's greatness the performance of God's promise, and let it encourage us to seek first the righteousness of God's kingdom. This was he, who, having tasted all earthly enjoyments, wrote a book, to show the vanity of all worldly things, the vexation of spirit that attends them, and the folly of setting our hearts upon them: and to recommend serious godliness, as that which will do unspeakably more to make us happy, that all the wealth and power he was master of; and, through the grace of God, it is within our reach.
10.m:
Solomon became very wealthy. Having lots of silver made it seem less valuable. The same happens with worldly wealth—having too much of it makes you think it’s worth less. And if having a lot of gold makes silver feel unimportant, shouldn’t understanding true wisdom and knowing about heaven make gold seem even less important?

We see God keeping His promises in Solomon’s success. This should encourage us to focus on what really matters—God’s righteousness and kingdom first. 

After experiencing all kinds of earthly pleasures, Solomon wrote a book showing how empty those things are. He explained how worrying about material goods causes stress and how silly it is to set your heart on them. 

His book also recommends being seriously devoted to God, because that brings greater happiness than all the wealth and power Solomon had. With God’s help, we can achieve this kind of devotion too.

11.o: There is not a more melancholy and astonishing instance of human depravity in the sacred Scriptures, than that here recorded. Solomon became a public worshipper of abominable idols! Probably he by degrees gave way to pride and luxury, and thus lost his relish for true wisdom. Nothing forms in itself a security against the deceitfulness and depravity of the human heart. Nor will old age cure the heart of any evil propensity. If our sinful passions are not crucified and mortified by the grace of God, they never will die of themselves, but will last even when opportunities to gratify them are taken away. Let him that thinks he stands, take heed lest he fall. We see how weak we are of ourselves, without the grace of God; let us therefore live in constant dependence on that grace. Let us watch and be sober: ours is a dangerous warfare, and in an enemy's country, while our worst foes are the traitors in our own hearts. (1Ki 11:9-13)
11.m:
This is one of the saddest examples in the Bible of how bad people can become. Solomon started worshipping false gods! He probably got used to being proud and living luxuriously, which made him lose interest in true wisdom. Nothing stops the trickery and wrongness inside us all by itself. Old age won't fix our bad habits either. Unless God’s help kills our sinful desires, those desires won’t go away even if there are no chances to act on them. 

Someone who feels safe should be careful not to slip up. We see how weak we are without God’s help. So we need to depend on God’s kindness all the time. We must stay alert and keep control of ourselves. Our battle against sin is hard and we fight in enemy territory, where our biggest enemies are the sins within us. (1Ki 11:9-13)

11.o: The Lord told Solomon, it is likely by a prophet, what he must expect for his apostacy. Though we have reason to hope that he repented, and found mercy, yet the Holy Ghost did not expressly record it, but left it doubtful, as a warning to others not to sin. The guilt may be taken away, but not the reproach; that will remain. Thus it must remain uncertain to us till the day of judgment, whether or not Solomon was left to suffer the everlasting displeasure of an offended God.
11.m:
The Lord probably told Solomon through a prophet what would happen because of his turning away from God. Even though we might think Solomon repented and received forgiveness, the Holy Spirit didn’t write it down clearly. This leaves it unclear if Solomon got mercy, which serves as a warning to others not to sin.

Even if someone’s sins are forgiven, the shame stays. We won’t know until Judgment Day if Solomon faced God’s anger forever or not.

11.o: While Solomon kept close to God and to his duty, there was no enemy to give him uneasiness; but here we have an account of two. If against us, he can make us fear even the least, and the very grasshopper shall be a burden. Though they were moved by principles of ambition or revenge, God used them to correct Solomon.
11.m: While Solomon stayed close to God and did what was right, he had no enemies. But now we hear about two who troubled him. Even the smallest thing can cause fear if God allows it. For example, a tiny insect could seem heavy. These enemies might have been motivated by greed or anger, but God still used them to teach Solomon a lesson.

11.o: In telling the reason why God rent the kingdom from the house of Solomon, Ahijah warned Jeroboam to take heed of sinning away his preferment. Yet the house of David must be supported; out of it the Messiah would arise. Solomon sought to kill his successor. Had not he taught others, that whatever devices are in men's hearts, the counsel of the Lord shall stand? Yet he himself thinks to defeat that counsel. Jeroboam withdrew into Egypt, and was content to live in exile and obscurity for awhile, being sure of a kingdom at last. Shall not we be content, who have a better kingdom in reserve?
11.m:
When God took the kingdom away from Solomon’s family, Ahijah told Jeroboam to be careful not to lose what he had been given. But God still needed to keep David’s family strong because the Messiah would come from them. 

Solomon tried to get rid of his successor. Didn’t he know that people cannot stop God’s plans? He thought he could, but he couldn’t.

Jeroboam went to live in Egypt for a while. He was okay with living far from home and not having much power, knowing he would become king someday. Shouldn’t we be happy too, since we have an even better future waiting for us?

11.o: Solomon's reign was as long as his father's, but his life was not so. Sin shortened his days. If the world, with all its advantages, could satisfy the soul, and afford real joy, Solomon would have found it so. But he was disappointed in all, and to warn us, has left this record of all earthly enjoyments, “Vanity and vexation of spirit.” The New Testament declares that one greater than Solomon is come to reign over us, and to possess the throne of his father David. May we not see something of Christ's excellency faintly represented to us in this figure?
11.m:
Solomon ruled for as long as his dad did, but he didn't live as long because of sin. If the world could truly satisfy someone and bring true happiness, Solomon would have been happy. But he wasn’t satisfied by anything, and wrote about how empty and frustrating worldly pleasures are. 

The New Testament tells us that someone even better than Solomon now rules over us and sits on David’s throne. Can we see some of Jesus' greatness shown through this comparison?

12.o: The tribes complained not to Rehoboam of his father's idolatry, and revolt from God. That which was the greatest grievance, was none to them; so careless were they in matters of religion, if they might live at ease, and pay no taxes. Factious spirits will never want something to complain of. And when we see the Scripture account of Solomon's reign; the peace, wealth, and prosperity Israel then enjoyed; we cannot doubt but that their charges were false, or far beyond the truth. Rehoboam answered the people according to the counsel of the young men. Never was man more blinded by pride, and desire of arbitrary power, than which nothing is more fatal. God's counsels were hereby fulfilled. He left Rehoboam to his own folly, and hid from his eyes the things which belonged to his peace, that the kingdom might be rent from him. God serves his own wise and righteous purposes by the imprudences and sins of men. Those that lose the kingdom of heaven, throw it away, as Rehoboam, by wilfulness and folly.
12.m:
The tribes did not complain about Solomon's worship of idols or leaving God. The biggest problem meant nothing to them. They only cared about living comfortably without paying taxes. People who cause trouble always find something to complain about. When we look at what the Bible says about Solomon’s time, we see that Israel had peace, wealth, and success. So we know their complaints were either lies or greatly exaggerated.

Rehoboam replied to the people based on advice from the younger men. Pride and a love for having total control made him blind. This led to God letting Rehoboam follow his foolish ways. God allowed Rehoboam to miss out on seeing what would have helped him. This caused the kingdom to split from him. 

God uses our mistakes to achieve His plans. Just like Rehoboam lost the kingdom because of stubbornness and foolishness, people today lose the kingdom of heaven through similar faults.

12.o: The people speak unbecomingly of David. How soon are good men, and their good services to the public, forgotten ! These considerations should reconcile us to our losses and troubles, that God is the Author of them, and our brethren the instruments: let us not meditate revenge. Rehoboam and his people hearkened to the word of the Lord. When we know God's mind, we must submit, how much soever it crosses our own mind. If we secure the favour of God, not all the universe can hurt us.
12.m:
People talk badly about David. Good people and their helpful actions are often quickly forgotten! This should help us accept our problems if God causes them and uses others to carry them out. We shouldn't think about getting even.

Rehoboam and his people listened to what the Lord said. When we know God’s will, we need to follow it, even if it goes against what we want. If we have God on our side, nothing else can really harm us.

12.o: Jeroboam distrusted the providence of God; he would contrive ways and means, and sinful ones too, for his own safety. A practical disbelief of God's all-sufficiency is at the bottom of all our departures from him. Though it is probable he meant his worship for Jehovah the God of Israel, it was contrary to the Divine law, and dishonourable to the Divine majesty to be thus represented. The people might be less shocked at worshipping the God of Israel under an image, than if they had at once been asked to worship Baal; but it made way for that idolatry. Blessed Lord, give us grace to reverence thy temple, thine ordinances, thine house of prayer, thy sabbaths, and never more, like Jeroboam, to set up in our hearts any idol of abomination. Be thou to us every thing precious; do thou reign and rule in our hearts, the hope of glory.
12.m:
Jeroboam did not trust that God would take care of him. So he came up with plans, even sinful ones, to keep himself safe. Not believing that God can provide everything we need leads us away from God.

Even though Jeroboam probably wanted to honor the God of Israel, making images of Him went against God’s laws and showed disrespect. People might have felt less upset about worshipping the God of Israel through these images compared to being asked to worship other gods right away. But this still opened the door for them to start worshipping false gods.

Lord, help us respect your temple, your rules, and your places of prayer. Don’t let us, like Jeroboam, put anything else above you in our hearts. May you be the most important thing to us, ruling over our lives.

13.o: In threatening the altar, the prophet threatens the founder and worshippers. Idolatrous worship will not continue, but the word of the Lord will endure for ever. The prediction plainly declared that the family of David would continue, and support true religion, when the ten tribes would not be able to resist them. If God, in justice, harden the hearts of sinners, so that the hand they have stretched out in sin they cannot pull in again by repentance, that is a spiritual judgment, represented by this, and much more dreadful. Jeroboam looked for help, not from his calves, but from God only, from his power, and his favour. The time may come when those that hate the preaching, would be glad of the prayers of faithful ministers. Jeroboam does not desire the prophet to pray that his sin might be pardoned, and his heart changed, but only that his hand might be restored. He seemed affected for the present with both the judgment and the mercy, but the impression wore off. God forbade his messenger to eat or drink in Bethel, to show his detestation of their idolatry and apostacy from God, and to teach us not to have fellowship with the works of darkness. Those have not learned self-denial, who cannot forbear one forbidden meal.
13.m:
The prophet threatened the altar, which was a way of warning against the people who built it and used it for false worship. False worship won't last forever, but God’s word will always be around. 

This prediction clearly showed that King David’s family would keep supporting true religion even after the ten tribes couldn’t fight back anymore. 

If God makes someone stubborn because of their sins, it means they can’t turn away from those sins through repentance. This is a serious punishment, shown here by the prophet. 

Jeroboam thought he could get help only from God, not from the golden calves he made. One day, people who dislike the truth might want the prayers of good ministers. 

Jeroboam asked the prophet to pray that his arm would heal, but not that he would stop being sinful. For now, Jeroboam felt sorry about what had happened, but later he forgot all about it. 

God told His messenger not to eat or drink at Bethel to show how much He hated the false worship there. It teaches us to stay away from evil practices. 

People haven’t truly learned to deny themselves if they can’t skip just one meal that God said not to eat.

13.o: The old prophet's conduct proves that he was not really a godly man. When the change took place under Jeroboam, he preferred his ease and interest to his religion. He took a very bad method to bring the good prophet back. It was all a lie. Believers are most in danger of being drawn from their duty by plausible pretences of holiness. We may wonder that the wicked prophet went unpunished, while the holy man of God was suddenly and severely punished. What shall we make of this? The judgments of God are beyond our power to fathom; and there is a judgment to come. Nothing can excuse any act of wilful disobedience. This shows what they must expect who hearken to the great deceiver. They that yield to him as a tempter, will be terrified by him as a tormentor. Those whom he now fawns upon, he will afterwards fly upon; and whom he draws into sin, he will try to drive to despair.
13.m:
The old prophet’s actions show that he wasn’t truly godly. When things changed under King Jeroboam, he chose comfort over faith. 

He used a dishonest way to get the good prophet to return. His story was false. 

Believers often face temptation through clever excuses for wrong behavior. 

It’s strange that the evil prophet escaped punishment, while the faithful one faced quick and harsh consequences. 

We might ask why this happened. But God’s justice is beyond us. There will be final judgment someday. 

No excuse exists for disobeying on purpose. 

This teaches us what happens to those who listen to the big liar. 

Those who give in to his tricks will later fear his punishments. 

Now he acts friendly, but later he attacks. Whoever he leads into sin, he tries to scare into despair.

13.o: God is displeased at the sins of his own people; and no man shall be protected in disobedience, by his office, his nearness to God, or any services he has done for him. God warns all whom he employs, strictly to observe their orders. We cannot judge of men by their sufferings, nor of sins by present punishments; with some, the flesh is destroyed, that the spirit may be saved; with others, the flesh is pampered, that the soul may ripen for hell. Jeroboam returned not from his evil way. He promised himself that the calves would secure the crown to his family, but they lost it, and sunk his family. Those betray themselves who think to support themselves by any sin whatever. Let us dread prospering in sinful ways; pray to be kept from every delusion and temptation, and to be enabled to walk with self-denying perseverance in the way of God's commands.
13.m:
God gets upset when his people sin. No one is safe from punishment just because of their job, closeness to God, or good deeds. 

God tells everyone he uses to follow his rules carefully. We can't tell if someone is good or bad based on what happens to them. Sometimes, God lets the body suffer so the spirit can be saved. Other times, God lets people enjoy life now, but their souls will face worse later.

Jeroboam did not stop doing wrong things. He thought making golden calves would keep his family in power, but instead, it cost him everything. People who try to stay in control through sin only hurt themselves.

We should fear success in sinful paths. We need to ask God to protect us from being tricked or tempted. And we should pray to have the strength to obey God’s commands without giving in to selfish desires.

14.o: “At that time,” when Jeroboam did evil, his child sickened. When sickness comes into our families, we should inquire whether there may not be some particular sin harboured in our houses, which the affliction is sent to convince us of, and reclaim us from. It had been more pious if he had desired to know wherefore God contended with him; had begged the prophet's prayers, and cast away his idols from him; but most people would rather be told their fortune, than their faults or their duty. He sent to Ahijah, because he had told him he should be king. Those who by sin disqualify themselves for comfort, yet expect that their ministers, because they are good men, should speak peace and comfort to them, greatly wrong themselves and their ministers. He sent his wife in disguise, that the prophet might only answer her question concerning her son. Thus some people would limit their ministers to smooth things, and care not for having the whole counsel of God declared to them, lest it should prophesy no good concerning them, but evil. But she shall know, at the first word, what she has to trust to. Tidings of a portion with hypocrites will be heavy tidings. God will judge men according to what they are, not by what they seem to be.
14.m:
At that time, when Jeroboam was doing evil, his child got sick. When illness enters our homes, we should ask if there might be some specific sin causing the trouble, so we can learn from it and stop doing wrong.

It would have been better if he asked why God was punishing him, instead of just wanting to know his fate. Most people prefer hearing about their future rather than being told about their mistakes or duties.

He sent for Ahijah because the prophet had said he would become king. People often expect their ministers to give them comfort even though they don't deserve it due to their sins. 

Jeroboam sent his wife disguised, hoping the prophet would only tell her about her son’s health. Some people want their ministers to only share good news and avoid telling the truth about their faults.

But right from the start, she knew what kind of message she would receive. Learning you’re part of a group of hypocrites is hard to hear. God judges based on who someone really is, not how they pretend to be.

14.o: Whether we keep an account of God's mercies to us or not, he does; and he will set them in order before us, if we are ungrateful, to our greater confusion. Ahijah foretells the speedy death of the child then sick, in mercy to him. He only in the house of Jeroboam had affection for the true worship of God, and disliked the worship of the calves. To show the power and sovereignty of his grace, God saves some out of the worst families, in whom there is some good thing towards the Lord God of Israel. The righteous are removed from the evil to come in this world, to the good to come in a better world. It is often a bad sign for a family, when the best in it are buried out of it. Yet their death never can be a loss to themselves. It was a present affliction to the family and kingdom, by which both ought to have been instructed. God also tells the judgments which should come upon the people of Israel, for conforming to the worship Jeroboam established. After they left the house of David, the government never continued long in one family, but one undermined and destroyed another. Families and kingdoms are ruined by sin. If great men do wickedly, they draw many others, both into the guilt and punishment. The condemnation of those will be severest, who must answer, not only for their own sins, but for sins others have been drawn into, and kept in, by them.
14.m:
Whether we remember God’s kindness to us or not, he always keeps track. And if we forget to be thankful, he might remind us later to help us see how ungrateful we’ve been.

Ahijah told Jeroboam that the sick child would soon die, which was actually a kind act because it spared the boy more suffering.

In Jeroboam’s household, Baasha was the only person who cared about worshipping God correctly and didn’t like the false idols.

To show His power and love, God sometimes chooses to save someone even from a very bad family, especially if that person shows some devotion to Him.

The good people are taken away from troubles here on earth so they can enjoy heaven sooner.

When the best members of a family pass away, it usually means trouble is coming for the rest of the family and kingdom.

Their deaths may seem sad now, but it’s never a real loss for them.

It was hard on the family and kingdom when the good ones died, but it should teach everyone to turn back to God.

God warned that punishments would come to the people of Israel because they followed Jeroboam’s wrong ways of worship.

After leaving King David’s line, the rulers changed quickly. One would take over and destroy the other.

Families and nations get destroyed because of sin.

If important leaders do wrong things, they pull others down too.

People who lead others into sin will face extra judgment for it.

14.o: Here is no good said of Rehoboam, and much said to the disadvantage of his subjects. The abounding of the worst crimes, of the worst of the heathen, in Jerusalem, the city the Lord had chosen for his temple and his worship, shows that nothing can mend the hearts of fallen men but the sanctifying grace of the Holy Spirit. On this alone may we depend; for this let us daily pray, in behalf of ourselves and all around us. The splendour of their temple, the pomp of their priesthood, and all the advantages with which their religion was attended, could not prevail to keep them close to it; nothing less than the pouring out the Spirit will keep God's Israel in their allegiance to him. Sin exposes, makes poor, and weakens any people. Shishak, king of Egypt, came and took away the treasures. Sin makes the gold become dim, changes the most fine gold, and turns it into brass.
14.m:
Here is no praise given to Rehoboam, and there is much criticism about his people. 

The worst sins spread widely among the people in Jerusalem, even worse than those of other nations. This city was chosen by God for His temple and worship. It shows that only the grace of the Holy Spirit can improve our hearts. We should rely on this grace and pray for it each day—for ourselves and everyone else.

Even with the grandeur of their temple and the honor of their priests, the people did not stay faithful to God. Only the Holy Spirit can keep God’s people loyal to Him.

Sin harms anyone who commits it. It makes them vulnerable and weak. King Shishak of Egypt came and stole their treasures. Sin causes valuable things to lose their worth, turning pure gold into mere brass.

15.o: Abijam's heart was not perfect with the Lord his God; he wanted sincerity; he began well, but he fell off, and walked in all the sins of his father, following his bad example, though he had seen the bad consequences of it. David's family was continued as a lamp in Jerusalem, to maintain the true worship of God there, when the light of Divine truth was extinguished in all other places. The Lord has still taken care of his cause, while those who ought to have been serviceable thereto have lived and perished in their sins. The Son of David will still continue a light to his church, to establish it in truth and righteousness to the end of time. There are two kinds of fulfilling the law, one legal, the other by the gospel. Legal is, when men do all things required in the law, and that by themselves. None ever thus fulfilled the law but Christ, and Adam before his fall. The gospel manner of fulfilling the law is, to believe in Christ who fulfilled the law for us, and to endeavour in the whole man to obey God in all his precepts. And this is accepted of God, as to all those that are in Christ. Thus David and others are said to fulfil the law.
15.m:
Abijam’s heart wasn’t completely sincere with the Lord. He started out good, but then he changed and followed his father’s bad ways, even after seeing how badly those choices turned out.

David’s family kept the true worship of God alive in Jerusalem, even when people stopped worshipping properly everywhere else. 

The Lord continues to protect his cause, even if some people who should help don’t live right. The Son of David will always be a light for his church, guiding them in truth and justice until the end.

There are two ways to follow the law. One way is the legal way, where people try to keep every rule perfectly on their own. Only Jesus and Adam before he sinned did that.

Another way is through the gospel. This means believing in Christ, who kept the law for us, and trying our best to follow God’s rules. People who believe in Christ get credit for keeping the law this way.

So we say that David and others fulfilled the law because they believed in Christ.

15.o: Asa did what was right in the eyes of the Lord. That is right indeed which is so in God's eyes. Asa's times were times of reformation. He removed that which was evil; there reformation begins, and a great deal he found to do. When Asa found idolatry in the court, he rooted it out thence. Reformation must begin at home. Asa honours and respects his mother; he loves her well, but he loves God better. Those that have power are happy when thus they have hearts to use it well. We must not only cease to do evil, but learn to do well; not only cast away the idols of our iniquity, but dedicate ourselves and our all to God's honour and glory. Asa was cordially devoted to the service of God, his sins not arising from presumption. But his league with Benhadad arose from unbelief. Even true believers find it hard, in times of urgent danger, to trust in the Lord with all their heart. Unbelief makes way for carnal policy, and thus for one sin after another. Unbelief has often led Christians to call in the help of the Lord's enemies in their contests with their brethren; and some who once shone brightly, have thus been covered with a dark cloud towards the end of their days.
15.m:
Asa did what was right in the eyes of the Lord. Doing what is truly right means doing what pleases God. During Asa’s time, there was a period of reforming things that were wrong. He got rid of many bad practices. When he found idols in the royal family, he took them out. Reform should start at home. 

Asa respected his mother very much, yet he loved God even more. People with authority are blessed when they use it wisely. We need to stop doing wrong and start doing good. This means getting rid of anything that goes against God and dedicating everything we have to honor him.

Asa was fully committed to serving God without being presumptuous. However, his alliance with Benhadad came from doubt. True believers sometimes struggle to trust the Lord completely during dangerous times. Doubt leads people to rely on worldly strategies instead of faith, causing them to commit more sins.

Unbelief has caused some Christians to seek help from God’s enemies against other believers. Some who once served God faithfully ended up making mistakes later in life because of such doubts.

15.o: During the single reign of Asa in Judah, the government of Israel was in six or seven different hands. Observe the ruin of the family of Jeroboam; no word of God shall fall to the ground. Divine threatenings are not designed merely to terrify. Ungodly men execute the just judgments of God upon each other. But in the midst of dreadful sins and this apparent confusion, the Lord carries on his own plan: when it is fully completed, the glorious justice, wisdom, truth, and mercy therein displayed, shall be admired and adored through all the ages of eternity.
15.m: During Asa’s time as king in Judah, there were six or seven different rulers in Israel. Notice what happened to Jeroboam’s family—nothing could stop God’s words from coming true. When God warns us, he doesn’t just want to scare us. Wicked people often punish each other for their wrongdoings. Yet even during all the sin and chaos, God kept working out his plans. One day, everyone will see how fair, wise, truthful, and merciful those plans were. People will praise him forever.

16.o: This chapter relates wholly to the kingdom of Israel, and the revolutions of that kingdom. God calls Israel his people still, though wretchedly corrupted. Jehu foretells the same destruction to come upon Baasha's family, which that king had been employed to bring upon the family of Jeroboam. Those who resemble others in their sins, may expect to resemble them in the plagues they suffer, especially those who seem zealous against such sins in others as they allow in themselves. Baasha himself dies in peace, and is buried with honour. Herein plainly appears that there are punishments after death, which are most to be dreaded. Let Elah be a warning to drunkards, who know not but death may surprise them. Death easily comes upon men when they are drunk. Besides the diseases which men bring themselves into by drinking, when in that state, men are easily overcome by an enemy, and liable to bad accidents. Death comes terribly upon men in such a state, finding them in the act of sin, and unfitted for any act of devotion; that day comes upon them unawares. The word of God was fulfilled, and the sins of Baasha and Elah were reckoned for, with which they provoked God. Their idols are called their vanities, for idols cannot profit nor help; miserable are those whose gods are vanities.
16.m:
This chapter talks about the kingdom of Israel and the changes happening there. Even though Israel has become very corrupt, God still calls them his people. 

Jehu said that the same punishment would happen to Baasha’s family that Baasha brought on Jeroboam’s family. People who commit the same sins as others might face similar punishments, especially if they pretend to be against those sins while allowing them in themselves.

Baasha died peacefully and was given an honorable burial. This shows that there are punishments after death that we should fear more than anything else.

Elah’s story warns drunkards that death could catch them off guard. When someone is drunk, death can come easily. Drunk people often get sick from drinking too much and are more likely to be attacked by enemies or have other dangerous things happen to them. They don’t have time to pray or do anything good before dying.

The words of God came true, and Baasha and Elah faced the consequences of their sins. Their idols are called vanities because these idols can't help or benefit anyone. It’s sad when people worship things that can’t really help them.

16.o: When men forsake God, they will be left to plague one another. Proud aspiring men ruin one another. Omri struggled with Tibni some years. Though we do not always understand the rules by which God governs nations and individuals in his providence, we may learn useful lessons from the history before us. When tyrants succeed each other, and massacres, conspiracies, and civil wars, we may be sure the Lord has a controversy with the people for their sins; they are loudly called to repent and reform. Omri made himself infamous by his wickedness. Many wicked men have been men of might and renown; have built cities, and their names are found in history; but they have no name in the book of life.
16.m:
When people turn away from God, they end up hurting each other. Arrogant leaders often cause harm to others. Omri fought against Tibni for several years. Even if we don't fully understand how God guides nations and people, we can still learn important lessons from history. 

When cruel rulers take over one after another, and there are killings, plots, and civil wars, we know that God is upset with the people because of their sins. They need to stop sinning and start living right.

Omri became known for being very bad. Many evil people were strong and famous. They built cities, and their names are in history books, but they aren’t written in the book of life.

16.o: Ahab did evil above all that reigned before him, and did it with a particular enmity both against Jehovah and Israel. He was not satisfied with breaking the second commandment by image-worship, he broke the first by worshipping other gods: making light of lesser sins makes way for greater. Marriages with daring offenders also imbolden in wickedness, and hurry men on to the greatest excesses. One of Ahab's subjects, following the example of his presumption, ventured to build Jericho. Like Achan, he meddled with the accursed thing; turned that to his own use, which was devoted to God's honour: he began to build, in defiance of the curse well devoted to God's honour: he began to build, in defiance of the curse well known in Israel; but none ever hardened his heart against God, and prospered. Let the reading of this chapter cause us to mark the dreadful end of all the workers of iniquity. And what does the history of all ungodly men furnish, what ever rank or situation they move in, but sad examples of the same?
16.m:
Ahab did more evil than any king before him. He showed special hatred toward both Jehovah and Israel. 

He didn't just break the second commandment about idol worship—he went further and worshipped other gods too. Dismissing small sins often leads to bigger ones.

Marriage to people who boldly sin only encourages more wickedness and pushes someone to do even worse things.

One of Ahab’s followers tried to rebuild Jericho, copying Ahab’s bold behavior. Just like Achan, he used something meant for God’s honor for himself. This man started building despite knowing the curse placed on anyone who touched those things.

No one has ever defied God and gotten away with it. Reading this should remind us of the terrible fate of all evildoers. Whether rich or poor, powerful or weak, the stories of ungodly people always show the same sad results.

17.o: God wonderfully suits men to the work he designs them for. The times were fit for an Elijah; an Elijah was fit for them. The Spirit of the Lord knows how to fit men for the occasions. Elijah let Ahab know that God was displeased with the idolaters, and would chastise them by the want of rain, which it was not in the power of the gods they served to bestow. Elijah was commanded to hide himself. If Providence calls us to solitude and retirement, it becomes us to go: when we cannot be useful, we must be patient; and when we cannot work for God, we must sit still quietly for him. The ravens were appointed to bring him meat, and did so. Let those who have but from hand to mouth, learn to live upon Providence, and trust it for the bread of the day, in the day. God could have sent angels to minister to him; but he chose to show that he can serve his own purposes by the meanest creatures, as effectually as by the mightiest. Elijah seems to have continued thus above a year. The natural supply of water, which came by common providence, failed; but the miraculous supply of food, made sure to him by promise, failed not. If the heavens fail, the earth fails of course; such are all our creature-comforts: we lose them when we most need them, like brooks in summer. But there is a river which makes glad the city of God, that never runs dry, a well of water that springs up to eternal life. Lord, give us that living water! (1Ki 17:8-16)
17.m:
God fits people perfectly for the tasks He gives them. There was a time when Elijah was needed, and Elijah was just right for the job. God knows how to match people to their roles.

Elijah told King Ahab that God was angry at the people who worshiped false gods and would punish them by making it rainless. This punishment couldn't be stopped because these fake gods had no control over rain.

God told Elijah to hide away. When God asks us to be alone or quiet, we should follow His plan. If we can’t help others, we should wait patiently. And if we can’t do anything for God, we should rest calmly.

Ravens brought Elijah food. People who barely get enough each day should trust God’s care and believe He will provide what they need daily.

God could have sent angels instead, but He showed that even small animals can do His work effectively. 

Elijah lived like this for more than a year. Natural water ran out since it depended on normal weather, but the special food kept coming as promised.

When the sky stops giving, the land stops too. All the things we rely on can disappear when we need them most, like streams drying up in summer. But there’s a source of water that never dries up—a spring that leads to everlasting life. Lord, give us that true, lasting water! (1Ki 17:8-16)
